stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 23rd.
stocks.news:
Layoffs: Meta (META -2.31%) is laying off around 8,000 staff to focus on AI. Nike (NKE -1.99%) is also cutting about 1,400 workers. Add to that: Microsoft (MSFT -3.96%) is offering voluntary retirement to 7% of its U.S. workforce, roughly 8,750 employees. Three big names trimming headcount on the same day.

Inching up: Intel (INTC +2.31%) popped after hours after beating earnings expectations. The chipmaker is starting to grow again. Meanwhile Texas Instruments (TXN +19.43%) had its best quarter ever on AI-related sales.

New deal: Regeneron (REGN +2.60%) struck a "most favored nation" deal with the White House. The company will sell its cholesterol drug on TrumpRx, commit close to $10B to U.S. manufacturing, and give away its new hearing-loss therapy for free.

Clear to launch: AST SpaceMobile (ASTS -6.98%) just got the FCC green light for its direct-to-device satellite service.

economy.note: US 10-Year and Gold as of 4:00 PM ET April 23rd; WTI Crude reflects today's intraday move via investing.com.
economy.commentary:
WTI pulled back nearly 3% as traders priced in the extended ceasefire, even though Iran seized two container ships midweek. Gold fell for a second straight day despite the geopolitical noise. When gold falls while the headlines should push it up, the signal is usually dollar strength: higher rates, fewer expected cuts, money parking in Treasuries. The Fed is projecting only one more cut in 2026.

College class of 2025 is doing better than 2024: 77% landed a job within three months of graduation, up from 63%. The catch: 16% sent 20+ applications to get that offer. Internships mattered big time. Not a market mover, but a signal about first-time household formation, which ties back to our starter-home inventory story.

crypto.note: Numbers as of 4:00 PM ET on April 23rd.
crypto.commentary:
The U.S. is officially running its own blockchain node through the Indo-Pacific Command. Sold as cybersecurity, not mining. First time a U.S. military command shows up on-chain.

Party's over, literally. The second annual TRUMP coin dinner is coming up. Last year the coin was worth $12B in market cap. Today it's at $1.4B. Prices have fallen 95% from their January 2025 peak. Minimum $8,640 worth of coins to attend. The roster won't look like last year.

real_estate.homebuilder: Rates held flat at 6.32%. But the post-Iran-strike context is real: the 30-year hit 6.5% at the peak of the conflict, up from 5.99% the day before strikes began. That's about $130 per month extra on a $400K home. 62% of builders say fuel prices are pushing material costs higher, which is why D.R. Horton, Lennar, and KB Home all missed Q1 estimates this spring.
real_estate.commentary:
Total active inventory across the 15-city board is 2,779, up from 2,726 yesterday (+53). Fresh supply today came in at 232 new listings, much stronger than yesterday's 193. Pleasanton had a notable surge. Spring inventory is visibly building now.

📍 Alameda & Contra Costa Snapshot (04/24/26)

📍 Fremont: 27 new. Steady with 356 active, anchor of Alameda County.

📍 Union City: 10 new. Best print of the week.

📍 Newark: 7 new. Back up after yesterday's 3.

📍 Hayward: 20 new. Consistent flow along I-880.

📍 Milpitas: 7 new. Stronger fresh supply. 27 townhomes still pending.

📍 Pleasanton: 23 new. Biggest Tri-Valley jump today.

📍 Dublin: 17 new. Strong bounce from yesterday's 9.

📍 San Ramon: 22 new. Balanced and climbing.

📍 Livermore: 12 new. Steady.

📍 Castro Valley: 9 new. Stable.

📍 Danville: 22 new. Contra Costa strong again.

📍 San Leandro: 12 new. Consistent.

📍 San Lorenzo: 4 new. Minimal.

📍 Oakland: 40 new. Back up, volume leader.

Luxury is living it up. Luxury home prices across 100 major cities rose 3.2% in 2025, ahead of 2.9% for standard homes. Dubai is up 25% in a year and 200% over 5 years. Stateside, the entry price for the top 10% of U.S. homes hit $1.25M in March. For our Fremont and Tri-Valley agents, that $1.25M threshold is not a luxury cutoff. It's just a standard 3-bed detached. The top-tier resilience is a real story to tell.

For listing agents: The split story this week is luxury resilience plus builder weakness. That's an opening. If you're listing in the $1.25M to $2.5M band in Fremont, Pleasanton, or San Ramon, position against the "new construction premium has priced itself out" narrative. Your resale is the value play. And keep pre-inspecting. Buyers are no longer skipping that step.
